subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Appeal Routes — Court of Appeal vs Divisional Court
fact_pattern: A party loses a final judgment in the Superior Court of Justice in a commercial action where the amount in dispute is $350,000. The party wishes to appeal. Counsel must determine whether the appeal goes to the Divisional Court or the Court of Appeal.
call_of_question: Which court has jurisdiction to hear an appeal from a final order of the Superior Court of Justice where the amount in dispute exceeds $50,000?

options.A: The Divisional Court hears all appeals from the Superior Court of Justice regardless of the amount.
options.B: The Court of Appeal for Ontario has jurisdiction under s.6 of the Courts of Justice Act to hear appeals from final orders of the Superior Court where the amount in dispute exceeds $50,000.
options.C: The Supreme Court of Canada has direct jurisdiction to hear appeals from the Superior Court of Justice in commercial matters over $50,000.
options.D: The Court of Appeal and the Divisional Court share concurrent jurisdiction over all appeals from the Superior Court of Justice.

explanation: Under s.6(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, an appeal lies to the Court of Appeal from a final order of the Superior Court of Justice in a proceeding where the amount in dispute is more than $50,000. The Divisional Court's appellate jurisdiction under s.19 of the Courts of Justice Act is limited to appeals from final orders of the Superior Court where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000, and to interlocutory orders with leave. With $350,000 in dispute, the Court of Appeal is the correct appellate forum.
why_A_wrong: The Divisional Court does not hear all appeals from the Superior Court. Its jurisdiction under s.19 of the Courts of Justice Act is limited to final orders under $50,000 (and interlocutory orders with leave). The Court of Appeal has jurisdiction for final orders above $50,000.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 6 of the Courts of Justice Act gives the Court of Appeal jurisdiction over appeals from Superior Court final orders where the amount exceeds $50,000.
why_C_wrong: The Supreme Court of Canada does not have direct jurisdiction to hear appeals from the Superior Court of Justice. It hears appeals from provincial courts of appeal only, by leave.
why_D_wrong: The Court of Appeal and the Divisional Court do not share concurrent jurisdiction over all Superior Court appeals. Their respective jurisdictions are defined by the amount in dispute under ss.6 and 19 of the Courts of Justice Act.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Appeal Routes — Court of Appeal vs Divisional Court
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.6(1)(b); s.19; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61

tested_concepts: Court of Appeal jurisdiction — s.6 CJA over $50,000
Divisional Court jurisdiction — s.19 CJA under $50,000
correct appellate forum for final Superior Court orders

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Leave to Appeal — When Required and the Test
fact_pattern: A party wishes to appeal an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice. Counsel advises that leave to appeal is required. The party asks what test the appellate court applies when determining whether to grant leave to appeal from an interlocutory order.
call_of_question: Under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, what is the test for obtaining leave to appeal an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice to the Divisional Court?

options.A: Leave is granted as a matter of right for all interlocutory orders; the only requirement is that the appeal be filed within 30 days.
options.B: Leave is granted where the proposed appeal raises an arguable point of law of general importance that ought to be decided by an appellate court, or there are conflicting decisions that need to be resolved.
options.C: Leave is granted only where the interlocutory order causes irreparable harm to the moving party pending trial.
options.D: Leave to appeal is not available for interlocutory orders; only final orders of the Superior Court may be appealed.

explanation: Under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, an appeal lies to the Divisional Court from an interlocutory order of the Superior Court of Justice, with leave. Courts have consistently applied a test for leave that asks whether the proposed appeal raises an arguable point of law of general importance to the administration of justice, or whether there are conflicting decisions among trial courts that ought to be resolved, or whether the order is clearly wrong. The test is designed to filter out appeals that do not raise issues of broader importance while permitting genuine legal questions to proceed.
why_A_wrong: Leave to appeal interlocutory orders is not granted as of right. Section 19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act requires the moving party to obtain leave, applying a discretionary test.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The test for leave to appeal an interlocutory order asks whether the appeal raises an arguable point of law of general importance or presents conflicting decisions that need resolution.
why_C_wrong: Irreparable harm is a factor in interlocutory injunction motions, not the test for leave to appeal an interlocutory order. The leave test focuses on legal importance and arguability.
why_D_wrong: Interlocutory orders are appealable to the Divisional Court with leave under s.19(1)(b) of the Courts of Justice Act. Leave to appeal is available for interlocutory orders.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Leave to Appeal — When Required and the Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.6(1)(b); s.19(1)(b)

tested_concepts: leave to appeal interlocutory orders — s.19(1)(b) CJA
test for leave — arguable point of general importance
filtering interlocutory appeals

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Standard of Review on Appeal — Questions of Law vs Fact
fact_pattern: On appeal, the appellant argues that the trial judge made two errors: (1) incorrectly applying the legal test for breach of fiduciary duty — a question of law; and (2) incorrectly finding that the defendant was aware of the plaintiff's reliance — a finding of fact. Counsel must advise on the appropriate standard of review for each issue.
call_of_question: Under Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, what standard of review applies to questions of law on appeal compared to questions of fact?

options.A: Both questions of law and questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of correctness on appeal.
options.B: Questions of law are reviewed on a standard of reasonableness, while questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of correctness.
options.C: Questions of law are reviewed on a standard of correctness; questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of palpable and overriding error, which is a highly deferential standard.
options.D: Both questions of law and questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of palpable and overriding error.

explanation: Under the standard of review framework established by the Supreme Court of Canada in Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, questions of law are reviewed on correctness — the appellate court owes no deference to the trial judge's legal conclusions and may substitute its own view. Questions of fact are reviewed on a standard of palpable and overriding error — the appellate court must identify a clear, plain, and determinative error that has affected the outcome before overturning a factual finding. This is a highly deferential standard that reflects the trial judge's privileged position as fact-finder.
why_A_wrong: Only questions of law are reviewed on correctness. Questions of fact attract the more deferential palpable and overriding error standard under Housen v. Nikolaisen.
why_B_wrong: The standards are reversed in this option. Questions of law are reviewed on correctness (not reasonableness); questions of fact are reviewed on palpable and overriding error (not correctness).
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Under Housen v. Nikolaisen: questions of law are reviewed on correctness; questions of fact are reviewed on the deferential palpable and overriding error standard.
why_D_wrong: Questions of law are not reviewed on palpable and overriding error. Only factual findings attract this deferential standard. Legal questions are reviewed on correctness under Housen.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Standard of Review on Appeal — Questions of Law vs Fact
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61

tested_concepts: Housen v. Nikolaisen 2002 SCC 33 — standards of review
questions of law — correctness standard
questions of fact — palpable and overriding error

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Standard of Review — Mixed Questions of Fact and Law
fact_pattern: On appeal, the appellant challenges the trial judge's determination that the defendant's conduct constituted negligence. The trial judge applied the correct legal test for negligence (duty, breach, causation, damage) but the appellant argues the judge misapplied the test to the specific facts found. Counsel must identify the applicable standard of review.
call_of_question: Under Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, what standard of review applies to mixed questions of fact and law?

options.A: Mixed questions of fact and law are always reviewed on a correctness standard because they involve an element of legal reasoning.
options.B: Mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on the palpable and overriding error standard as a default; however, if a legal error can be extracted and isolated from the factual findings, that extracted legal question is reviewed on correctness.
options.C: Mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on the reasonableness standard, giving deference to the trial judge's balancing of legal and factual considerations.
options.D: Mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on a balance of probabilities standard by the appellate court.

explanation: Under the framework in Housen v. Nikolaisen, 2002 SCC 33, mixed questions of fact and law are reviewed on palpable and overriding error as the default standard, because such questions are typically dominated by factual findings and trial courts are best positioned to make them. However, where an extricable legal question can be isolated from the factual findings — for example, where the trial judge has incorrectly defined the legal test or applied an incorrect legal principle — that extracted legal question is reviewed on correctness. The challenge in the fact pattern involves the application of a correct legal test to facts, which is a classic mixed question reviewed on palpable and overriding error absent an extricable legal error.
why_A_wrong: Mixed questions of fact and law are not always reviewed on correctness. The default is palpable and overriding error; correctness applies only where an extricable legal question can be isolated.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The default standard for mixed questions is palpable and overriding error, with correctness applying only to extricable legal questions isolated from the factual findings.
why_C_wrong: Reasonableness is the standard in administrative law review, not in civil appellate review. Housen v. Nikolaisen does not apply a reasonableness standard to mixed questions in civil appeals.
why_D_wrong: Balance of probabilities is a standard of proof at trial, not a standard of appellate review. Appellate courts do not re-weigh evidence on a balance of probabilities standard.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Standard of Review — Mixed Questions of Fact and Law

tested_concepts: mixed questions default — palpable and overriding error
extricable legal question reviewed on correctness
Housen v. Nikolaisen 2002 SCC 33

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Fresh Evidence on Appeal — Palmer Test
fact_pattern: After losing at trial, the appellant discovers a document that was not produced at trial and now seeks to introduce it on appeal as fresh evidence. The document was in the possession of a third party and was not obtained by the appellant before trial, although the appellant acknowledges that a request for it could have been made during discovery.
call_of_question: Under the Palmer test for the admission of fresh evidence on appeal, which criterion is the appellant most likely to fail to satisfy?

options.A: The credibility criterion — the fresh evidence must be credible and probably accurate.
options.B: The relevance criterion — the fresh evidence must be relevant to a decisive issue at trial.
options.C: The due diligence criterion — the fresh evidence could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence before trial; the appellant's failure to request the document during discovery means this criterion is not met.
options.D: The decisive criterion — the fresh evidence must be conclusive on a key issue and is therefore too speculative to meet the standard.

explanation: Under the Palmer test, derived from Palmer v. The Queen, [1980] 1 SCR 759 and applied in Ontario civil appeals, fresh evidence must satisfy four criteria to be admitted on appeal: (1) due diligence — the evidence could not have been obtained with reasonable diligence before trial; (2) relevance — the evidence must be relevant to a decisive issue; (3) credibility — the evidence must be apparently credible; and (4) decisiveness — the evidence must be such that if believed it could reasonably be expected to have affected the result. In the scenario, the appellant acknowledges that a request for the document could have been made during discovery. This failure to exercise due diligence is fatal to the first Palmer criterion and will likely result in refusal to admit the fresh evidence.
why_A_wrong: The credibility criterion requires the fresh evidence to be apparently credible. This is often the easiest Palmer criterion to satisfy for a document, as documents are generally credible on their face. The due diligence criterion is the one most likely to be failed here.
why_B_wrong: The relevance criterion requires the evidence to relate to a decisive issue. Whether the document is relevant depends on its content, but the fact pattern does not suggest it lacks relevance. The due diligence failure is the critical problem.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The Palmer test requires due diligence — the evidence must not have been obtainable with reasonable effort before trial. Acknowledging the document could have been requested during discovery defeats this criterion.
why_D_wrong: The decisive criterion requires that the fresh evidence, if believed, could reasonably be expected to have affected the result. This is a reasonable threshold, not a requirement that the evidence be conclusive or certain. The due diligence failure is the critical barrier.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Fresh Evidence on Appeal — Palmer Test
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Palmer v. The Queen, [1980] 1 SCR 759; Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61

tested_concepts: Palmer test for fresh evidence on appeal
four criteria: due diligence, relevance, credibility, decisiveness
due diligence — failure to request in discovery is fatal

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Stay of Execution Pending Appeal — Test and Procedure
fact_pattern: Nakamura Electronics Inc. loses a commercial judgment for $800,000 at trial and intends to appeal to the Court of Appeal. Nakamura asks its counsel whether it must pay the judgment or whether it can stay execution pending appeal. Counsel must advise on the applicable test for obtaining a stay.
call_of_question: What is the test an appellant must satisfy to obtain a stay of execution of a money judgment pending appeal in Ontario?

options.A: The filing of a notice of appeal automatically stays execution of the judgment pending the appeal; no motion is required.
options.B: To obtain a stay of execution pending appeal, the moving party must satisfy the three-part test from RJR-MacDonald: (1) there is a serious question to be tried (or a non-frivolous appeal); (2) the moving party will suffer irreparable harm if the stay is not granted; and (3) the balance of convenience favours granting the stay.
options.C: A stay of execution pending appeal is available only for injunctions and equitable orders, not for money judgments.
options.D: To obtain a stay, the appellant need only demonstrate that the appeal is not frivolous; no other factors are considered.

explanation: Under r.63.02 of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194) and the established test drawn from RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311, a party seeking a stay of execution of a judgment pending appeal must satisfy three criteria: (1) a serious question to be tried — whether the appeal raises a non-frivolous or arguable issue; (2) irreparable harm — the moving party will suffer irreparable harm if the stay is not granted and must pay the judgment before the appeal is resolved; and (3) balance of convenience — which party will suffer greater harm from granting or refusing the stay. The filing of a notice of appeal alone does not automatically stay a money judgment; a motion for a stay is required. Courts weigh all three factors and have discretion to impose conditions, such as requiring security.
why_A_wrong: Filing a notice of appeal does not automatically stay execution of a money judgment in Ontario. A motion for a stay of execution is required, and the appellant must satisfy the three-part test.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. A stay of execution pending appeal requires satisfying the three-part RJR-MacDonald test: serious question, irreparable harm, and balance of convenience.
why_C_wrong: Stays of execution are available for money judgments as well as equitable orders. The RJR-MacDonald test applies broadly to stay motions pending appeal in civil proceedings.
why_D_wrong: A non-frivolous appeal alone is insufficient to obtain a stay. The moving party must also demonstrate irreparable harm and that the balance of convenience favours a stay. All three parts of the test must be addressed.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Stay of Execution Pending Appeal — Test and Procedure
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.63.02; RJR-MacDonald Inc v Canada (AG), [1994] 1 SCR 311

tested_concepts: stay of execution pending appeal — r.63.02
three-part RJR-MacDonald test
notice of appeal does not automatically stay judgment

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Divisional Court Appeal — Final Orders Under $50,000 and Statutory Appeals
fact_pattern: A claimant obtains a final judgment against a contractor for $38,000 in the Superior Court of Justice. The contractor wishes to appeal the judgment. Counsel must identify the correct court to which the appeal lies.
call_of_question: Under s.19 of the Courts of Justice Act, which court hears appeals from final orders of the Superior Court of Justice where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000?

options.A: The Court of Appeal for Ontario, as it has jurisdiction over all appeals from the Superior Court of Justice.
options.B: The Divisional Court, which under s.19(1)(a) of the Courts of Justice Act hears appeals from final orders of the Superior Court where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000.
options.C: The Small Claims Court, as a final judgment of $38,000 falls within its monetary jurisdiction.
options.D: The Ontario Court of Justice (formerly Provincial Court), which hears all monetary claims under $50,000.

explanation: Under s.19(1)(a) of the Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, an appeal lies to the Divisional Court from a final order of the Superior Court of Justice made without a jury in a proceeding where the amount in dispute does not exceed $50,000. With a final judgment of $38,000, the amount in dispute falls below the $50,000 threshold and the appeal lies to the Divisional Court, not the Court of Appeal. The Court of Appeal's jurisdiction under s.6 is reserved for final orders where the amount in dispute exceeds $50,000.
why_A_wrong: The Court of Appeal does not have jurisdiction over all appeals from the Superior Court. Under ss.6 and 19 of the Courts of Justice Act, the appellate forum is determined by the amount in dispute: under $50,000 goes to the Divisional Court; over $50,000 goes to the Court of Appeal.
why_B_wrong: This IS the correct answer. Section 19(1)(a) of the Courts of Justice Act gives the Divisional Court jurisdiction over appeals from final Superior Court orders where the amount does not exceed $50,000.
why_C_wrong: The Small Claims Court is a trial court, not an appellate court. Appeals from Small Claims Court judgments go to the Divisional Court. The Small Claims Court does not hear appeals from the Superior Court.
why_D_wrong: The Ontario Court of Justice does not hear appeals from the Superior Court of Justice. It is a separate trial-level court with its own jurisdiction. Appeals from Superior Court final orders go either to the Divisional Court or the Court of Appeal depending on the amount.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Divisional Court Appeal — Final Orders Under $50,000 and Statutory Appeals
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Courts of Justice Act, R.S.O. 1990, c. C.43, s.19(1)(a)

tested_concepts: s.19(1)(a) CJA — Divisional Court for final orders under $50,000
contrast with Court of Appeal jurisdiction under s.6
correct appellate forum based on amount in dispute

subject: civil_litigation
subtopic_heading: Notice of Appeal — Time to Appeal and Extending Time
fact_pattern: A final judgment was released by the trial judge on March 1. The losing party's counsel did not file a notice of appeal within the prescribed time period. On June 10, more than three months later, counsel brings a motion to extend the time to appeal. Counsel argues that the delay was due to an administrative error in the office and that the appeal has merit.
call_of_question: Under r.61.04 of the Rules of Civil Procedure, what is the time to deliver a notice of appeal from a final order, and what factors does a court consider when deciding whether to extend the time to appeal?

options.A: The time to appeal is 60 days from the date of the order; an extension will be granted automatically upon a showing of administrative error.
options.B: The time to appeal is 30 days from the date of the order; the court has no power to extend the time to appeal once it has passed.
options.C: The time to appeal is 30 days from the date of the order under r.61.04(1); the court may extend time under r.3.02 considering factors including the length of the delay, the reason for the delay, the prejudice to the respondent, and whether the appeal has merit.
options.D: The time to appeal is 15 days from the date of the order; after 15 days the right to appeal is permanently forfeited and cannot be reinstated.

explanation: Under r.61.04(1) of the Rules of Civil Procedure (R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194), a notice of appeal to the Court of Appeal shall be served and filed within 30 days of the date of the order appealed from. Where a party has not filed within 30 days, the court may extend the time to appeal under r.3.02. Courts consider several factors in deciding whether to extend time: (1) the length of the delay; (2) the reason for the delay (whether it was inadvertent or deliberate); (3) whether the intended appellant has shown that the appeal has merit (i.e., a prima facie ground for the appeal); (4) prejudice to the respondent from granting the extension; and (5) whether refusing the extension would cause a substantial injustice. An extension is not automatic even for an administrative error — the court considers all factors in the interests of justice.
why_A_wrong: The time to appeal is 30 days, not 60 days. An extension is also not automatic upon showing an administrative error; the court weighs all relevant factors.
why_B_wrong: The court does have power to extend the time to appeal under r.3.02. The expiry of the appeal period does not permanently extinguish the right to appeal; it requires a court order extending time.
why_C_wrong: This IS the correct answer. The time to appeal is 30 days under r.61.04(1); extensions are available under r.3.02 upon consideration of delay length, reason, prejudice, and merit.
why_D_wrong: The time to appeal is 30 days, not 15 days. The right to appeal is not permanently and irrevocably forfeited after 15 days; an extension of time may be sought under r.3.02.

source_reference.subject: Civil Litigation
source_reference.heading: Notice of Appeal — Time to Appeal and Extending Time
source_reference.rule_or_statute_reference: Rules of Civil Procedure, R.R.O. 1990, Reg. 194, r.61.04(1); r.3.02

tested_concepts: 30-day time to appeal — r.61.04(1)
extension of time — r.3.02
factors for extension: delay, reason, merit, prejudice
